text: This video depicts a robotic excision of left adrenal hemorrhagic cyst in a pregnant lady. A twenty-four year old lady had presented with nausea and vomiting. She was found to have an eight point seven centimeter adrenal cyst and she was eight weeks pregnant. An EUSN biopsy did not show any malignant cells. CTA and MRI confirmed it to be a hemorrhagic cyst.
Because of persistent pain, she was taken to the operating room eight weeks later when she was seventeen weeks pregnant.

The surgeon enters the abdomen using an optical trocar and inserts robot trocars under direct vision. After docking the robot, the splenic flexure is mobilized and dissected from the kidney and spleen. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to assess vascularity, revealing no active bleeding and a discernible plane between the renal vessels and the aorta.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect tissues around the adrenal cyst, starting inferiorly and laterally. Final dissection is performed with scissors, approaching the cyst from the inferior, median, and superior aspects. The adrenal gland is identified on the superior lateral margin and dissection begins. A small feeding vessel into the spleen is encountered and promptly ligated.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral and inferior aspects of the edematous spleen, facilitating better separation. The superior dissection proceeds between the pancreas, allowing further mobilization of the spleen and improved dissection of the cyst.

The surgeon develops the plane between the adrenal gland and the cyst on the medial aspect, using a vessel sealer to address the superior pole. Bleeding occurs upon entering the gland, which is promptly controlled with a vessel sealer and bipolar cautery. The dissection continues from the medial aspect.

The surgeon controls bleeding from the cyst using bipolar cautery while dissecting the superior pole. The adrenal gland is mobilized from the medial and superior aspects, and all feeding vessels are taken. A large adrenal vein is dissected from the renal vessel, and posterior attachments are severed using hot scissors.
The surgeon places locking clips on the adrenal vein and ligates it using a vessel sealer. The vessel sealer is then used to dissect the remaining posterior attachments of the adrenal gland.
The surgeon places the cyst into an EndoCatch bag and extracts it through a small midline incision.
